slides.title: Introduction to Go (Golang)
slides.subtitle:
Simplicity, Reliability, and Efficiency
Modern Systems Programming

box.content: Why Go was created and what it stands for

Go (often called Golang) is a statically typed, compiled programming language designed at Google. It was created to address the challenges of large-scale software development: slow builds, uncontrolled dependencies, and difficult concurrency. Go combines the performance of low-level languages like C++ with the readability and productivity of high-level languages like Python.

list.text: Static Typing - Provides safety and catches errors early at compile time.
Garbage Collected - Relieves developers from manual memory management, preventing memory leaks.
Compiled - Direct translation to machine code means no virtual machine overhead, leading to blazing fast execution.

box.content: Created at Google in 2007 to solve real engineering problems

note.text: Released as open source in November 2009

list.text: Readability - Code is for humans to read

box.content: The Go Gopher is one of the most beloved mascots in tech
Setting up your workspace for success

list.text: Select installer for Windows, Mac, or Linux

box.content:
Before version 1.11, projects had to live inside a single global workspace defined by the GOPATH environment variable. This was restrictive and made versioning difficult.

Modern Go uses Go Modules (introduced in 1.11 and default since 1.13), which allows you to place your project anywhere and manage dependencies precisely with a go.mod file.

list.text: Legacy (GOPATH): Strictly required code to be at $GOPATH/src.
Modern (Modules): Dependencies are tracked per project, ensuring reproducible builds.
Command: Initialize with go mod init to start a new project.

box.content: Variables, Types, and Syntax

Every Go file starts with a package declaration. The main package is a special name that tells the Go compiler that this file should compile as an executable, rather than a shared library. The main function inside this package is the entry point where execution begins.

list.text: package main - Identifies the file as an executable program.
import "fmt" - Provides functions for formatting text and printing to console.
func main() - The mandatory starting point for any Go application.

box.content: Go is strongly typed, but it offers several ways to declare variables to reduce verbosity. While var is used for general declarations, the short declaration operator := is the most common way to initialize variables within functions.

note.text: Note: := cannot be used outside of function bodies.

box.content: Variables are initialized to their zero value if not specified
Go strings are UTF-8 encoded by default

note.text: A rune is a Unicode code point

box.content: Safe pointer support - No pointer arithmetic
box.code:
x := 42
p := &x      // Get address
fmt.Println(*p) // Dereference

*p = 21      // Change value at address

box.content: Go requires explicit conversion
Arrays, Slices, and Maps
Fixed size, part of the type

note.text: Arrays are rarely used directly; use slices!

box.content: Slices are the most powerful and common data structure in Go. Unlike arrays, which have a fixed size, slices are dynamic and grow as needed. A slice is essentially a descriptor for a contiguous segment of an underlying array.
Under the hood, a slice is a header consisting of three components. Understanding this is crucial for avoiding common bugs like 'unintended sharing' of underlying arrays between different slices.

list.text: Pointer: Points to the first element of the range in the underlying array.
Length: The number of elements currently in the slice.
Capacity: The maximum number of elements the slice can hold before needing to reallocate.

box.code:
s := make([]int, 5, 10) // len 5, cap 10

copy_s := make([]int, len(s))
copy(copy_s, s)

// Remove element at index i
s = append(s[:i], s[i+1:]...)

list.text: May allocate a new underlying array

box.content: A map is Go's built-in associative data structure (like a dictionary or hash table). It maps keys of one type to values of another. Keys must be 'comparable' types (integers, strings, etc.).
Warning: Iteration order is random!
Collections of fields
If, For, Switch
Go's if statement is familiar but has a few unique features. Parentheses are not required around the condition, and you can include a short 'initialization' statement before the condition.

note.text: The initialization variable is often used for error checking: if err := doTask(); err != nil

box.content: The only loop construct in Go
No default fallthrough - Cleaner than C
box.code:
switch os := runtime.GOOS; os {
case "darwin":
    fmt.Println("Mac")
case "linux":
    fmt.Println("Linux")
default:
    fmt.Println(os)
}

var i interface{} = 42
switch v := i.(type) {
case int:
    fmt.Println("Integer", v)
case string:
    fmt.Println("String", v)
}

box.content: Reusable logic and deferred execution

Functions are first-class citizens in Go. They can be passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and assigned to variables. A function is defined with the func keyword, followed by the name, parameters, and the return type.

The defer statement is one of Go's most elegant features for resource management. It delays the execution of a function until the surrounding function is about to return. It is commonly used for closing files, unlocking mutexes, or cleaning up resources, ensuring they are always handled regardless of which path the function takes to return.
Defining behavior for types
Go does not have classes, but you can define 'methods' on any type (usually structs). A method is just a function with a special 'receiver' argument between the func keyword and the method name.

list.text: Pointer receivers are more memory efficient

box.content: No inheritance - use Embedding

box.code:
p1 := Person{"Alice", 25}       // Positional
p2 := Person{Name: "Bob"}       // Named (Age=0)
p3 := new(Person)               // Pointer to zeroed
p4 := &Person{Name: "Charlie"}   // Preferred

box.content: Polymorphism through implicit implementation

In many languages, you must explicitly state that a class 'implements' an interface. In Go, this is done implicitly. If a type provides all the methods declared in an interface, it is considered to implement that interface automatically. This allows for very flexible and decoupled code.
interface{} (or any) can hold any value
box.code:
var i any
i = 42
i = "hello"

box.content: Errors are values, not exceptions

Go encourages handling errors explicitly rather than catching exceptions. Errors are just values that implement the error interface. Functions that can fail usually return an error as their last return value, and it's the caller's responsibility to check it immediately.
Only for unrecoverable situations

list.text: Recover: Regain control in a deferred function

box.content: Goroutines, Channels, and Select
Concurrency is not Paralellism

list.text: Concurrency: Dealing with many things at once

box.content: A goroutine is a lightweight thread of execution managed by the Go runtime. Unlike OS threads, which are heavy and have a large fixed stack size, goroutines start with very little memory (about 2KB) and are multiplexed onto a small number of OS threads. This allows you to run hundreds of thousands of goroutines simultaneously without exhausting system resources.

Channels are the pipes that connect concurrent goroutines. You can send values into channels from one goroutine and receive those values into another. By default, channels are unbuffered, meaning they require both the sender and receiver to be ready at the same time for the communication to occur. This provides a natural synchronization point.
Asynchronous until capacity is reached
box.code:
ch := make(chan int, 2)
ch <- 1
ch <- 2
// ch <- 3 would block

box.content: Waiting on multiple channel operations
box.code:
select {
case msg1 := <-c1:
    fmt.Println("Received", msg1)
case c2 <- 42:
    fmt.Println("Sent to c2")
default:
    fmt.Println("No activity")
}

box.content: Synchronizing multiple goroutines
Protecting shared state
box.code:
var mu sync.Mutex
mu.Lock()
counter++
mu.Unlock()

box.content: Find concurrency bugs easily
box.code:
go run -race main.go
go test -race ./...

box.content: Built-in power without dependencies

Go's standard library includes a production-grade HTTP server. It is highly performant because every request is handled in its own goroutine automatically. This makes Go an excellent choice for building microservices and web APIs.

box.code:
data, _ := json.Marshal(p)
json.Unmarshal(data, &p)

f, _ := os.Open("file")
b, _ := io.ReadAll(f)

box.content: Timeouts and Cancellation
box.code:
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, 5 * time.Second)
defer cancel()

box.content: First-class testing support
Generics and beyond
